source: Thematic Investors — Blending sci-fi, history and literature
summary: xAI is the most compelling private AI company due to unique data (X/Twitter), compute, and distribution
summary_extended: Baker argues that xAI is the most compelling private AI company because it possesses a uniquely valuable combination of proprietary data, compute, and distribution that few competitors can replicate. Specifically, he highlights X/Twitter's human debate data — including Community Notes as a crowdsourced truth mechanism — as exceptionally valuable for training AI models and for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), given X's large active user base. Baker views xAI as one of only four viable foundation model companies globally, alongside Google, Meta, and the OpenAI/Microsoft partnership, suggesting the market for frontier AI will consolidate around a small number of well-resourced players. He notes xAI has raised $6 billion, giving it the capital to compete on compute, and that its distribution through X provides a built-in feedback loop that most startups lack. The investment implication is primarily relevant to private market investors, as xAI is not publicly listed, though the thesis implicitly frames Meta and Google as the publicly accessible proxies for viable foundation model competition.
detail: X's human debate data — including Community Notes as a crowdsourced truth mechanism — is uniquely valuable for training AI models. xAI has raised $6B, has access to this proprietary data, and has distribution through X's user base for RLHF. Baker views xAI as one of only four viable foundation model companies alongside Google, Meta, and OpenAI/Microsoft.
confidence_evidence: Baker expresses clear conviction but acknowledges xAI is private and the outcome is uncertain; he frames it as having the right ingredients rather than guaranteeing success
quote_fragment: xai is a private company and it has access to X's data which is really unique and valuable

ticker_audit.reasoning: Baker explicitly names Meta as one of only four viable foundation model companies alongside xAI, Google, and OpenAI/Microsoft. Meta is a publicly listed equity that is a direct subject of this competitive framing and benefits from the same structural thesis about consolidation among frontier AI players.

Baker explicitly names Google as one of only four viable foundation model companies. As a publicly listed equity and named participant in the foundation model oligopoly thesis, Google is a direct beneficiary of the consolidation narrative Baker is articulating.

Baker explicitly names OpenAI/Microsoft as one of the four viable foundation model companies. Microsoft is the publicly listed vehicle for OpenAI exposure and is directly named in the competitive framing of this thesis.
